headline: Butte Miners Launch General Strike, 1919
body:
The air in Butte, Montana, on February 8, 1919, was thick not just with the acrid scent of copper dust, but with the simmering resentment of twelve thousand men. These were the miners, the muscle and sinew of the Anaconda Copper Mining Company, a behemoth that clawed one-sixth of the world's copper from the earth beneath the "Richest Hill." For years, Anaconda had held Butte in its iron grip, a company town where a man's very right to toil was dictated by the infamous "rustling card." This was no mere employment pass; it was a blacklist, a tool of corporate control that ensured only the compliant found work, and those who dared to speak of fair wages or safe conditions found themselves cast into the bitter Montana winter.

The Great War had ended, and with it, the insatiable demand for copper that had kept the mines running at a fever pitch. Now, the company, ever vigilant for profit, saw its chance. A dollar-per-day wage cut was announced, a cruel blow to men already living on the precipice of poverty. This was the spark that ignited the powder keg. The miners, many hardened by years of struggle and imbued with the radical spirit of the Industrial Workers of the World, would not stand for it. They had seen their brothers crushed by rockfalls, poisoned by gas, and exploited by a system that valued ore over human life. The Anaconda Company, which in 1918 had employed 75 percent of the district's mine-working population, now faced a united front.

The strike that erupted was not a polite negotiation; it was a declaration of war against a system designed to keep them in perpetual servitude. The city's vital copper production ground to a halt. The miners demanded not only the rescinding of the wage cut but, more profoundly, the abolition of the rustling card system itself. As Paul F. Brissenden noted in his 1920 article, "The Butte Miners and the Rustling Card," the system had "elicited passionate protest and emphatic demands for its abolition." The struggle was long and arduous, a testament to the unyielding spirit of those who dared to challenge the industrial titans. The fight for economic justice in Butte was a microcosm of a larger battle raging across America, a battle for the dignity of labor against the relentless machinery of capital.
